text: In adolescence, synaptic pruning reaches its most dramatic pitch. The prefrontal cortex — the seat of planning, impulse control, and judgment — undergoes extensive restructuring between the ages of twelve and twenty-five, a process that depends critically on adequate sleep. Neuroscientist Sarah-Jayne Blakemore has documented how this period of pruning sharpens the signal-to-noise ratio of the adolescent brain, eliminating unused connections while strengthening those that are exercised. Chronic sleep restriction during this window does not merely impair performance in the short term; it may permanently alter the architecture of a brain still under construction, with consequences that persist into adulthood.
question: According to the passage, why is sleep deprivation in adolescence more consequential than in adulthood?

options.text: Adolescents produce more cortisol during wakefulness, accelerating synaptic degradation
The adolescent brain requires more total sleep hours to sustain basic metabolic function
Sleep loss in adolescence may permanently alter brain architecture still under active construction
The hippocampus does not consolidate memories effectively until the prefrontal cortex is fully myelinated
